jokes.text: Hey, George... the ocean called. They're running out of shrimp.
jokes.setup: George is presumably eating a lot of shrimp at a business meeting
jokes.punchline: Absurd premise that George's consumption could affect ocean shrimp supply
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld absurdist humor - takes George's gluttony to ridiculous extreme. Perfectly economical and became culturally iconic.

jokes.text: Oh, yes. That's what I should have said.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests a comeback to the shrimp line
jokes.punchline: George's delayed realization and classic inability to think on his feet
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for George - his perpetual 'l'esprit de l'escalier' (thinking of comebacks too late) is core to his character.

jokes.text: Damn it!
jokes.setup: George's realization he missed his comeback opportunity
jokes.punchline: His frustrated outburst at his own inadequacy
jokes.explanation: Simple character beat - George's frustration is relatable but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah? Well, the jerk store called, and they're running out of you.
jokes.setup: George repeats the ocean/shrimp insult to set up his comeback
jokes.punchline: His mirror-structure comeback with 'jerk store'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant comedic construction - mirrors the original insult's structure while being completely absurd. 'Jerk store' became a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Really? That's great. You said that to him? / Well, actually, I thought it up on the way over here.
jokes.setup: Jerry's enthusiasm for George's comeback
jokes.punchline: George admits he didn't actually say it when it mattered
jokes.explanation: Classic deflation of George's moment - the admission undercuts the triumph perfectly, very true to his character.

jokes.text: A can of balls for the nice guy.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks for tennis balls in normal way
jokes.punchline: Milos's overly formal, awkward phrasing reveals his foreignness
jokes.explanation: Mild character humor - Milos's formal speech pattern is mildly amusing but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: You don't plan to hit these balls with that racket, do you?
jokes.setup: Jerry has presumably a bad/old racket
jokes.punchline: Milos's condescending question, ironic given he's terrible at tennis
jokes.explanation: Sets up the irony of Milos being a tennis snob despite being awful - gains humor on rewatch when you know he's terrible.

jokes.text: Vincent is an art-house goon. / Gene? Oh, he's so stupid and mainstream.
jokes.setup: Discussion of video store employee recommendations
jokes.punchline: Their snobbish dismissals of each other's taste
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing their petty cultural snobbery, though not exceptional.

jokes.text: That makes me the premiere.
jokes.setup: Learning a movie went straight to video
jokes.punchline: Kramer's misuse of 'premiere' thinking he's the first viewer
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer malapropism - his confident misunderstanding of language is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I, Cosmo Kramer... hereby want Jerry Seinfeld to remove my life support... feeding machine, lung blower, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera.
jokes.setup: Kramer explaining his living will
jokes.punchline: His casual, informal language for serious medical equipment
jokes.explanation: 'Lung blower' is wonderfully Kramer-esque - treating life support equipment like household appliances.

jokes.text: Jackie Chiles, he put a restraining order on me. I'm not allowed within 200 feet of his office. I couldn't even give him his Christmas present.
jokes.setup: Kramer needs a lawyer
jokes.punchline: His own lawyer has a restraining order against him, but he's still considerate about Christmas
jokes.explanation: Beautifully absurd - only Kramer could have his own lawyer restraining him, yet still worry about gift-giving etiquette.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, I put all your Z's on the weights-and-measures page.
jokes.setup: Jerry notices his address book Z page is missing
jokes.punchline: Kramer's bizarre organizational system
jokes.explanation: Pure Kramer logic - his completely irrational filing system presented as if it's sensible.

jokes.text: Who is this? / It's Vincent. / Of Vincent's picks? / The same.
jokes.setup: Elaine gets an unexpected call
jokes.punchline: The video store clerk identifying himself by his movie recommendations
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist moment - Vincent's self-identification through movie picks is charmingly odd.

jokes.text: Your screening process is getting ever more rigorous.
jokes.setup: Elaine talked to Vincent for two hours without meeting him
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic observation about Elaine's dating standards
jokes.explanation: Sharp sarcasm - Jerry noting the irony that Elaine will talk to strangers for hours but claims to be selective.

jokes.text: George used up all my guest passes already.
jokes.setup: Question about why they're playing at a crummy court
jokes.punchline: George's typical abuse of privileges
jokes.explanation: Standard George characterization - his tendency to exploit friendships for personal gain.

jokes.text: He could have played just as well with a log.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining Milos's terrible tennis skills
jokes.punchline: The absurd comparison to playing with a log
jokes.explanation: Good visual image - comparing racket to log emphasizes both the equipment scam and Milos's incompetence.

jokes.text: Kramer wants to die with dignity. / There's a feather in your cap.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining Kramer's living will request
jokes.punchline: Elaine's sarcastic response about what an honor it would be
jokes.explanation: Dark humor handled well - Elaine's dry response to being asked to 'pull the plug' on a friend.

jokes.text: I don't wanna be a vegetable, Elaine. I just want out.
jokes.setup: Discussion of living wills
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual, almost cheerful attitude toward death
jokes.explanation: Kramer's matter-of-fact approach to mortality is darkly funny and very much in character.

jokes.text: Your cranium called. It's got some space to rent.
jokes.setup: Trying to help George with comeback alternatives
jokes.punchline: Jerry's attempt at the same insult structure, calling George stupid
jokes.explanation: Weaker attempt at the format - trying too hard to be clever and the rental metaphor doesn't quite land.

jokes.text: Hey, Reilly, the zoo called. You're due back by 6.
jokes.setup: Another attempt at the comeback format
jokes.punchline: Kramer's simple animal insult
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - Kramer's directness works better than Jerry's over-cleverness.

jokes.text: Look, just tell him you had sex with his wife. That'll get him.
jokes.setup: Discussion of comeback strategies
jokes.punchline: Elaine's nuclear option suggestion
jokes.explanation: Dark and direct - gains significance when George actually uses this line later. Elaine's practical cruelty.

jokes.text: This is why I hate writing with a large group. Everybody has their own opinions, and it all gets homogenized... and you lose the whole edge of it.
jokes.setup: Everyone offering George comeback alternatives
jokes.punchline: George's pretentious analysis of creative process, as if comeback lines are high art
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-moment - George treating his stupid comeback as if it's a screenplay, revealing his grandiosity.

jokes.text: It's still got spring in the strings.
jokes.setup: Kramer questions Jerry taking a racket from garbage
jokes.punchline: Jerry's pathetic justification for his cheapness
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment - Jerry's frugality leading to rationalization of using garbage equipment.

jokes.text: Trust me, Kramer, given the legal opportunity, I will kill you.
jokes.setup: Kramer doubts Jerry's commitment to executing the living will
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact assurance about killing his friend
jokes.explanation: Perfectly deadpan dark humor - Jerry's casual commitment to euthanasia as a favor to a friend.

jokes.text: You're a calculating, cold-hearted business woman. When there's dirty work to be done, you don't mind stomping on throats.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why Elaine is perfect for the living will task
jokes.punchline: His brutally honest assessment of her personality as qualifications
jokes.explanation: Jerry's assessment of Elaine as ruthlessly practical is both insulting and presented as praise.

jokes.text: Yank it like you're starting a mower.
jokes.setup: Discussing pulling the plug on life support
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual, mechanical comparison for ending his life
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Kramer reducing death to starting lawn equipment shows his unique worldview perfectly.

jokes.text: You have liver, kidneys and gallbladder... but no central nervous system. / Well, I gotta have a central nervous system.
jokes.setup: Going through hypothetical medical scenarios
jokes.punchline: Kramer's matter-of-fact rejection based on losing his essential Kramer-ness
jokes.explanation: Kramer's priorities are revealed - he can lose organs but not his neurological uniqueness.

jokes.text: I'd stick. / Yeah, yeah, stick, because I could still go to the coffee shop.
jokes.setup: Scenario where you can eat but machines do everything else
jokes.punchline: Their decision based entirely on coffee shop access
jokes.explanation: Perfect priorities - reducing life-or-death decisions to maintaining social routines at Monk's Cafe.

jokes.text: Believe me, it is Milos' great shame... but, Jerry, I could lose my business if anybody find out.
jokes.setup: Milos admitting his tennis incompetence
jokes.punchline: His overwrought concern about reputation despite running a tennis pro shop while being terrible
jokes.explanation: The irony of a tennis professional being terrible at tennis, though not exceptionally executed.

jokes.text: Game, set and match, huh, Milos?
jokes.setup: Jerry presumably just dominated Milos in tennis
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic celebration of beating someone who claims to be a pro
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic victory moment - Jerry rubbing in his easy win over the 'pro.'

jokes.text: An old woman experiences pain and yearning. / A hundred and ninety-two minutes?
jokes.setup: Reading pretentious movie description
jokes.punchline: Jerry's horror at the runtime for such a minimal plot
jokes.explanation: Great observational humor - the disconnect between art film pretension and audience patience.

jokes.text: That's a lot of yearning, huh?
jokes.setup: The 192-minute runtime
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dry assessment of yearning per minute
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - reducing pretentious filmmaking to a quantifiable measurement of yearning.

jokes.text: Weekend at Bernie's II. Now, that's an hilarious premise.
jokes.setup: Gene recommending lowbrow comedy
jokes.punchline: His sincere enthusiasm for a sequel about a dead guy
jokes.explanation: Mild irony - Gene's genuine appreciation for obviously stupid comedy, setting up the contrast with Vincent's pretension.

jokes.text: Bernie is dead, you morons. Just because he's wearing sunglasses, he looks alive?
jokes.setup: Elaine watching Weekend at Bernie's II
jokes.punchline: Her frustrated logic about the movie's absurd premise
jokes.explanation: Elaine's intellectual frustration with stupid comedy - her inability to just enjoy mindless entertainment.

jokes.text: How long is this weekend, anyway?
jokes.setup: Continuing to watch Bernie sequel
jokes.punchline: Logical question about the movie's timeline
jokes.explanation: Good observational point about sequels - how long can one weekend's events be stretched?

jokes.text: A Gene pick. How could you? I thought we had something special. / No, it doesn't mean anything. I'm not even gonna rewind it.
jokes.setup: Vincent calling about Elaine's movie choice betrayal
jokes.punchline: Treating video store recommendations like infidelity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist romance - Vincent's jealousy over movie choices and Elaine's 'not rewinding' as a sign of disrespect.

jokes.text: We only wake you up for the important meetings.
jokes.setup: George asking about Reilly leaving
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that George sleeps through most meetings
jokes.explanation: Perfect George characterization - his colleagues' matter-of-fact acceptance of his laziness.

jokes.text: Why don't we just go up to your apartment?
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting coffee
jokes.punchline: Patty's immediate suggestion to skip to his apartment
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for Jerry's awkward situation - woman being more forward than expected.

jokes.text: I feel so rested and refreshed. / Get me a toothbrush.
jokes.setup: Woman waking up from coma
jokes.punchline: Her immediate concern about dental hygiene after extended unconsciousness
jokes.explanation: Amusing priority after coma - focusing on mundane hygiene rather than medical marvel of recovery.

jokes.text: Enough talk, Jerry. / Not for me. I love chatting.
jokes.setup: Patty trying to seduce Jerry
jokes.punchline: Jerry's preference for conversation over physical intimacy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry - his preference for neurotic conversation over simple physical pleasure is quintessential character comedy.

jokes.text: Milos, my husband.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking who she's doing this for
jokes.punchline: Revelation that creates the medieval sexual payola situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect plot twist - two words that completely recontextualize the situation and create absurdist comedy gold.

jokes.text: He chose to offer you his wife as some sort of medieval sexual payola.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining Milos's scheme
jokes.punchline: The perfect description of wife-offering as business transaction
jokes.explanation: 'Medieval sexual payola' is a brilliant comedic construction - combining historical reference with modern corruption terminology.

jokes.text: Because of society, right? / Yes, George, because of society.
jokes.setup: George asking why Jerry didn't sleep with Patty
jokes.punchline: Jerry's weary confirmation of basic social norms preventing adultery
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - George's need to have basic morality explained, Jerry's exhausted response.

jokes.text: 'Jerk store' would have smoked that guy. Smoked him, I say.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying George never had the right comeback
jokes.punchline: George's increasingly emphatic defense of his line
jokes.explanation: George's obsessive defense of his comeback, adding 'smoked him, I say' shows his theatrical pretensions.

jokes.text: Oh, I wanted to see that. Thanks. Thanks a lot.
jokes.setup: Elaine spoiling the coma movie ending
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic thanks for the spoiler
jokes.explanation: Standard spoiler reaction - not particularly clever but functionally serves the plot.

jokes.text: I didn't know it was possible not to know that.
jokes.setup: Kramer not knowing people can wake from comas
jokes.punchline: Elaine's incredulity at such basic ignorance
jokes.explanation: Elaine's sharp response to Kramer's ignorance - the double negative construction adds wit.

jokes.text: Gene's trash. / I'm Gene.
jokes.setup: Elaine dismissing Gene's movie picks
jokes.punchline: Gene revealing himself, creating awkward moment
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe setup and payoff - two words that create maximum awkwardness. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: I guess that's a risk you run when you dabble in the flesh trade.
jokes.setup: Milos complaining his wife doesn't respect him
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact analysis of prostituting your wife
jokes.explanation: 'Dabble in the flesh trade' is perfectly constructed - treating wife-offering as casual business venture.

jokes.text: I'll do it as long as there's no other girls around. I mean, I wanna be a man too.
jokes.setup: Milos asking Jerry to let him win at tennis
jokes.punchline: Jerry's condition that his emasculation be private
jokes.explanation: Jerry's compromise - willing to help but protecting his own masculinity. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Boy, look how far back it goes. It's like a tooth.
jokes.setup: Examining the VCR play button Vincent sent
jokes.punchline: Comparing the button to a tooth with roots
jokes.explanation: Visual observation that's mildly amusing but not particularly sharp or memorable.

jokes.text: So guess where Mr. Ocean Phone turned up. / He's working for Firestone in Akron, Ohio.
jokes.setup: George tracking down Reilly
jokes.punchline: The specific, mundane location where his nemesis ended up
jokes.explanation: 'Mr. Ocean Phone' is a great nickname, and George tracking someone to Ohio for a comeback shows his obsession.

jokes.text: You're flying to Akron just to zing a guy?
jokes.setup: George's plan to fly to Ohio
jokes.punchline: Jerry's incredulous summary of the absurd mission
jokes.explanation: 'Flying to Akron just to zing a guy' perfectly captures the absurdity of George's quest.

jokes.text: See, there are no jerk stores.
jokes.setup: Jerry critiquing George's comeback
jokes.punchline: Pointing out the logical flaw in the jerk store concept
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on the joke itself - Jerry as comedy critic pointing out the absurdist logic.

jokes.text: It's smart. It's a smart line, and a smart crowd will appreciate it. And I'm not gonna dumb it down for some bonehead mass audience.
jokes.setup: Jerry saying the line is confusing
jokes.punchline: George defending his comeback as high art for intellectuals
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - George's pretentious defense of a stupid joke as if it's avant-garde comedy. Perfect grandiosity.

jokes.text: You're strange and beautiful and sensitive.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to convince Vincent to meet her
jokes.punchline: Her romantic description of someone she's never seen
jokes.explanation: Gains humor knowing Vincent is a teenager with acne - Elaine's romantic projection onto a voice.

jokes.text: Oh, Mr. Kramer, you had a 10:30 appointment. It's 2:00. Mr. Shellbach had a tennis lesson. He's gone for the day.
jokes.setup: Kramer arriving late for his appointment
jokes.punchline: The lawyer prioritizing tennis over death-related legal work
jokes.explanation: Ironic priorities - lawyer choosing recreation over client's existential legal needs.

jokes.text: He's not a man, this Jerry. He's not even married like I am.
jokes.setup: Milos trying to impress his wife by dominating Jerry
jokes.punchline: Using bachelorhood as an insult while having just pimped out his wife
jokes.explanation: Ironic masculinity display - Milos using marriage as a virtue while having just offered his wife for sex.

jokes.text: Hey, everybody, look. The little chicken girl wants me to ease up.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking Milos to tone down the insults
jokes.punchline: Milos's increasingly theatrical emasculation of Jerry
jokes.explanation: Milos going overboard with the humiliation, 'chicken girl' is a colorfully emasculating insult.

jokes.text: He cries like a woman.
jokes.setup: Milos continuing to humiliate Jerry publicly
jokes.punchline: The ultimate emasculating insult
jokes.explanation: Generic emasculating insult - serves the plot but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: No, my acne!
jokes.setup: Vincent trying to hide from Elaine
jokes.punchline: Revelation that the sophisticated movie buff is a teenager with acne
jokes.explanation: Excellent reveal - Vincent's sophistication was all projection, he's just an insecure teenager. Great comedic structure.

jokes.text: Vodka, cigarettes, fireworks. What kind of a sick woman brings this to a 15-year-old?
jokes.setup: Vincent's mother discovering what Elaine brought
jokes.punchline: The inappropriate nature of the 'romantic' supplies for a teenager
jokes.explanation: Great payoff - recontextualizing Elaine's romantic gestures as corrupting a minor. Good moral outrage.

jokes.text: We have the same taste in movies.
jokes.setup: Elaine being confronted about bringing adult items to teenager
jokes.punchline: Her pathetic justification for the inappropriate relationship
jokes.explanation: Elaine's desperate justification for what looks like corrupting a minor - her cultural snobbery as defense.

jokes.text: Look at the big baby. Hey, big baby, are you wetting yourself? Maybe it is time for you to be changed.
jokes.setup: Milos continuing to humiliate Jerry at tennis
jokes.punchline: Escalating from 'chicken girl' to literal baby insults
jokes.explanation: Escalation of emasculation but not particularly clever - more quantity than quality of insults.

jokes.text: The baby got lucky on that one.
jokes.setup: Jerry presumably makes a good shot
jokes.punchline: Milos dismissing Jerry's success as luck while maintaining baby metaphor
jokes.explanation: Continuing the baby theme but it's getting repetitive - diminishing returns on the humiliation.

jokes.text: Let's see how many I can fit in my mouth.
jokes.setup: George at the meeting with shrimp
jokes.punchline: His gluttonous challenge that sets up the original insult scenario
jokes.explanation: Sets up the recreation of the original scenario - George's competitive eating creates the opening for insult.

jokes.text: You know, George... the ocean called. They're running out of shrimp.
jokes.setup: The recreated scenario with George eating shrimp
jokes.punchline: Reilly delivering the exact same line that started this whole quest
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the payoff - identical recreation of the original scenario George has obsessed over.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, Reilly? Well, the jerk store called. They're running out of you.
jokes.setup: The moment George has been waiting for
jokes.punchline: Finally delivering his perfect comeback in the right moment
jokes.explanation: The climactic moment - George's quest fulfilled. The line works perfectly in context and his delivery is triumphant.

jokes.text: What's the difference? You're their all-time bestseller.
jokes.setup: George's jerk store comeback
jokes.punchline: Reilly's devastating counter-comeback that works within George's metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed comeback - uses George's own jerk store logic against him. 'All-time bestseller' is perfectly devastating.

jokes.text: Yeah? Well, I had sex with your wife.
jokes.setup: George being destroyed by the bestseller line
jokes.punchline: His desperate resort to Elaine's nuclear option
jokes.explanation: George's desperation move - falling back on Elaine's cruel suggestion when his perfect line fails.

jokes.text: His wife's in a coma.
jokes.setup: George's sex boast
jokes.punchline: The revelation that makes George's insult horrifically inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy payoff - three words that destroy George completely. Connects to coma subplot brilliantly. Maximum impact.

jokes.text: 'My wife's in a coma.' Yeah? Well, the life-support machine called and.... Stupid.
jokes.setup: George trying to think of a comeback to the coma revelation
jokes.punchline: His attempt to use the format fails completely and he knows it
jokes.explanation: George's continued obsession with the format even in defeat - his immediate self-recognition of failure is perfect.

jokes.text: Wait. Yes. That's what I should have said.
jokes.setup: George still trying to find the perfect comeback
jokes.punchline: The return of his eternal 'that's what I should have said' problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect circular ending - George right back where he started, trapped in his eternal comeback cycle.

jokes.text: You're meat, Reilly! You just screwed yourself!
jokes.setup: George thinking he's found his comeback
jokes.punchline: His delusional victory declaration despite having been completely destroyed
jokes.explanation: George's final delusion - declaring victory after his most humiliating defeat. Pure character consistency.
